coredata.dc:title: Facile preparation of fluorescent polydihydroxyphenylalanine nanoparticles for label-free detection of copper ions
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Su, Yubin
Shi, Bingfang
Liao, Suqi
Qin, Yingfeng
Zhang, Liangliang
Huang, Mengjiao
Zhao, Shulin
coredata.dc:description: In this work, we developed an effective and convenient fluorescent Cu2+ detection method using polydihydroxyphenylalanine nanoparticles (PDNPs) as nanoprobes. The fluorescent PDNPs were facilely prepared by autopolymerization of dihydroxyphenylalanine under alkaline condition. The fluorescence of the PDNPs could be quenched by Cu2+. The PDNP nanoprobe is low cost and simple, and does not require further post-modification with Cu2+ specific recognition units. In addition, this nanoprobe exhibits high sensitivity and the detection limit is well below the safety limit of Cu2+ in drinking water permitted by the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency. Furthermore, the sensing system is also easy to implement, fast, and selective. Satisfactory detection of Cu2+ in river water samples were observed, demonstrating the potential practical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Polydihydroxyphenylalanine nanoparticles
Copper ions
Fluorescence detection
Nanoprobe

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence quenching of PDNPs by Cu2+
Assay performance of PDNPs for Cu2+ detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the preparation of fluorescent PDNPs (route A) and the detection of Cu2+ (route B).

(A) Time dependent UV–vis absorption spectra of DOPA solution (1mM) after the addition of NaOH (1M). (B) FTIR spectra of DOPA and formed PDNPs. (C) SEM image of the synthesized PDNPs. (D) Size distribution of the obtained PDNPs.
(A) Photographs of the obtained PDNPs solution (3.4mg/mL) under visible light (left) and 365nm UV light (right). (B) Excitation wavelength dependent fluorescence spectra of 0.34mg/mL PDNPs.

(A) The fluorescence spectra of PDNPs (0.34mg/mL) with or without 30μM Cu2+. Inset is the photographs of 3.4mg/mL PDNPs solution in the absence (a) and presence of 150μM Cu2+ (b). (B) The fluorescence quenching efficiency of various metal ions. The concentration of each metal ion was 3.0μM, and the PDNPs concentration was 0.34mg/mL.
Fluorescence intensity-based Stern–Volmer plot. The concentration of PDNPs was 0.34mg/mL.

(A) The effect of pH on fluorescence quenched efficiency after adding 10μM Cu2+. The concentration of PDNPs was 0.34mg/mL. (B) Fluorescence quenching of 0.34mg/mL PDNPs by 3.0μM Cu2+ in HEPES buffer (pH=7.5) as a function of reaction time.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of PDNPs (0.34mg/mL) in the presence of Cu2+ at various concentrations. The Cu2+ concentrations from a to m were 0.10, 0.50, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0, 2.5, 3.0, 4.0, 5.0, 7.0, 10, 20 and 30μM. (B) The relationship between (F 0 − F)/F 0 and Cu2+ concentration. Inset is the linear range for Cu2+ assay. Error bars were estimated from three replicate measurements.
Recovery experiments of Cu2+ detection in river samples.

head.ce:title: Facile preparation of fluorescent polydihydroxyphenylalanine nanoparticles for label-free detection of copper ions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory for the Chemistry and Molecular Engineering of Medicinal Resources of Education Ministry, Guangxi Normal University, Guilin 541004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for the Chemistry and Molecular Engineering of Medicinal Resources of Education Ministry, Guangxi Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry and Life Sciences, Baise University, Baise 533000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and Life Sciences, Baise University

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These two authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, we developed an effective and convenient fluorescent Cu2+ detection method using polydihydroxyphenylalanine nanoparticles (PDNPs) as nanoprobes. The fluorescent PDNPs were facilely prepared by autopolymerization of dihydroxyphenylalanine under alkaline condition. The fluorescence of the PDNPs could be quenched by Cu2+. The PDNP nanoprobe is low cost and simple, and does not require further post-modification with Cu2+ specific recognition units. In addition, this nanoprobe exhibits high sensitivity and the detection limit is well below the safety limit of Cu2+ in drinking water permitted by the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency. Furthermore, the sensing system is also easy to implement, fast, and selective. Satisfactory detection of Cu2+ in river water samples were observed, demonstrating the potential practical applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, heavy metal ion contamination is an important public health concern [1]. As an essential trace element in the human body, copper plays an important role in various biological processes. It is a cofactor of many metalloenzymes, and crucial in energy generation, dioxygen transport and activation, and signal transduction [2]. However, high Cu2+ levels that exceed cellular needs are toxic to living organisms, inducing gastrointestinal, hepatic, and renal system disorders, as well as neurodegenerative diseases [3–5]. Furthermore, the widespread use of Cu2+ also poses serious environmental problems. Hence, the detection of copper is of great importance to human health and ecosystems.

Up to now, numerous methods have been developed for Cu2+ detection, including traditional instrumental analysis methods (such as atomic absorption spectroscopy, inductivity coupled plasma mass spectroscopy, and inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectroscopy) [6–8], organic dye-based fluorescent probes [9,10], electrochemical assays [11,12], and more recently, nanomaterial-based fluorescent and colorimetric methods [13–18]. Among these methods, using fluorescent nanoparticles, especially metallic quantum dots and carbon dots [17–22], to detect Cu2+ has increasingly drawn research interest. However, the synthesis of metallic quantum dots is tedious and requires strict synthesis conditions. In addition, the preparation of carbon dots, which commonly utilizes a hydrothermal method or pyrolysis approach, requires a high reaction temperature. Furthermore, for most of these fluorescent nanoprobes, post-modification with Cu2+-specific recognition units is needed to improve the selectivity, making the nanoprobe fabrication complicated.

Polydopamine, a novel multifunctional material which has a similar structure to the eumelanin polymers, has been widely used in surface modification of various substrates, catalysis, cancer diagnosis, drug delivery, and biochemical sensing [23]. Recently, few metal ion detection methods were reported using polydopamine or its derivative materials as recognition probes [24,25]. Ma et al. developed a colorimetric sensor for Cu2+ using dopamine-coated silver nanoparticles [24]. In this method, Cu2+ had to be mixed with dopamine before the polymerization of dopamine, leading to a complicated implementation. Recently, Zhang et al. [26] and Yildirim et al. [27] reported that the polydopamine nanoparticles also display an intrinsic high fluorescence. This property may allow the construction of polydopamine nanoparticle-based fluorescent probes for metal ion sensing. However, at present, only Xiong et al. have developed a dopamine-derived oligomer as a fluorescent probe for Fe3+ detection [25]. A long synthesis time and post-modification of the fluorescent probe were required in their work.

Herein, we facilely prepared fluorescent polydihydroxyphenylalanine nanoparticles (PDNPs) for label-free Cu2+ detection. Dihydroxyphenylalanine (DOPA) is the precursor of dopamine in biological processes [28]. Similar to polydopamine [27], we speculated that DOPA will also be oxidized to its quinone derivative and then autopolymerized to form PDNPs in alkaline solution. In this work, we prepared fluorescent PDNPs by simply adding NaOH to a DOPA solution under stirring at room temperature (Scheme 1 , route A). It has been reported that the fluorescence of many luminescent nanoparticles, such as metallic quantum dots and carbon dots, can be quenched by Cu2+ through a complexation or collisional deactivation process [17–22,29–31]. Additionally, according to previous reports, DOPA can strongly bind with Cu2+ through multi-coordination with N and O atoms in the amino, carboxyl and catechol groups of DOPA molecule, and the binding affinity is higher than for other metal ions [32–36]. Inspired by these phenomena, we speculate that the fluorescence of PDNPs may be specifically quenched by Cu2+ upon the contact between Cu2+ and fluorescent PDNPs. Thus the prepared PDNPs could sever as a label free fluorescent probe for Cu2+ recognition, since the PDNPs were prepared by the DOPA and did not require post-modification with Cu2+ recognition units (Scheme 1, route B). The developed nanoprobe here is simple, low cost, fast, sensitive, selective, and environmentally friendly. This method not only provides a novel route for Cu2+ detection, but also expands the application field of polydopamine derivative materials.

3,4-Dihydroxy-l-phenylalanine, CuSO4 and other metal ions were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Sodium hydroxide and 2-[4-(2-Hydroxyethyl)-1-piperazinyl]ethanesulfonic acid (HEPES) were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All reagents are of analytical grade and were used without further purification. Deionization water (DI water, 18.2MΩcm) was purified by employing a Milli-Q plus 185 water purification system (Bedford, MA).

Absorption measurement was performed with a Cary 60 UV–vis spectrometer (Agilent Technologies). Fluorescence spectra were recorded on a Perkin-Elmer LS55 luminescence spectrometer (Perkin-Elmer, USA). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) study was conducted from KBr pellets on a Perkin-Elmer FT-IR spectrophotometer (Perkin-Elmer, USA). Fluorescence lifetime experiments were recorded on time-resolved fluorescence spectrometer FL3-P-TCSPC (HORIBA JOBIN JVON, France). Scanning electron microscope (SEM) images were obtained on environmental scanning electron microscope FEI Quanta 200 FEG (Philips, Netherlands).

PDNPs were synthesized according to previous method for polydopamine preparation with a slight change [27]. Briefly, 100μL of NaOH (1M) was added into 4.9mL of dihydroxyphenylalanine solutions (1mM) in HEPES buffer (10mM, pH=7.5). The mixture was stirred at room temperature (∼25°C) for 3h. After that, 1M HCl was added to stop the polymerization reaction. The pH of the final solution was about 7.0. Finally, the obtained PDNPs were purified using membrane dialysis. A stock solution of PDNPs at a concentration of 3.4mg/mL was prepared by dissolving the appropriate amount in deionized water, and it was stored at 4°C for use.

The obtained PDNPs stock solution was diluted to 0.34mg/mL with HEPES buffer solution (10mM, pH=7.5). Then, 20μL of as-diluted PDNP solution and Cu2+ solution with various concentrations were added into the HEPES buffer solution to give a the final volume of 200μL. After incubation for 10min, the fluorescence intensities were recorded in the wavelength range of 490–590nm. The slot widths of the excitation and emission were both 15nm, and the excitation wavelength was set at 415nm. The fluorescence quenching efficiency, i.e. the ratio of (F 0 − F)/F 0 (F and F 0 were the fluorescence intensities of PDNPs at 520nm in the presence and absence of Cu2+, respectively), was used to evaluate the assay performance. All measurements were conducted in triplicate, and the standard deviation was plotted as error bars.

To test the practicality, the proposed method was applied to analysis the real water sample obtained from the Lijiang River of Guilin (Guangxi province, China). The samples were pretreated according to previous work [37]. The river water was filtered through a 0.22μm membrane and then centrifuged at 12,000rpm for 20min. The obtained final samples were spiked with Cu2+ standard solutions with different concentrations and then analysis using the developed PDNP nanoprobes.

DOPA will be oxidized to the quinone derivative under alkaline conditions and then autopolymerized to form PDNPs. The oxidization of DOPA and formation of PDNPs were investigated using UV–vis spectroscopy, as shown in Fig. 1A. After the addition of NaOH, a peak appeared at around 420nm, indicating the formation of the quinone derivative of DOPA [38]. The peak at around 320nm, which emerged with an increase in oxidation time, may be the intermediate products in the polymerization of DOPA, such as 5,6-dihydroxyindole-2-carboxylic acid and dopachrome [38]. Additionally, the absorption at all wavelengths between 400 and 700nm increased gradually as the time increased, demonstrating the formation of PDNPs [27,39].

The FTIR spectra of DOPA and the formed PDNPs are displayed in Fig. 1B. Clear differences in the FTIR spectra of DOPA and the PDNPs can be observed, demonstrating the formation of PDNPs. The FTIR spectrum of the PDNPs is similar to that of melanin [40]. The broad and intense peak centered at 3433cm−1 was attributed to OH vibrations. The peaks at 1636cm−1 and 1460cm−1 were due to the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of the COO− group, respectively. The band at 1382cm−1 was attributed to the CNC stretching vibrations. The above results indicate the presence of carboxyl, imine and hydroxy groups. These functional groups not only improve the hydrophilicity of the PDNPs in aqueous systems, but may also act as Cu2+ binding sites.

Furthermore, the formed PDNPs were also confirmed using scanning electron microscopy (SEM), as shown in Fig. 1C. The particle size distribution was given in Fig. 1D. It can be seen that the sizes of the PDNPs are broad in the range from 90 to 220nm, and the average size is about 160nm.

Under irradiation with 365nm UV light, green fluorescence of the PDNP solution was clearly observed (Fig. 2A). The fluorescence spectra of the PDNPs are shown in Fig. 2B. With increasing the excitation wavelength from 355 to 455nm, the maximum emission of the PDNPs only slightly shifts to longer wavelengths (about 10nm). This is different from previous observations of fluorescent polydopamine, which showed a large red shift in peak wavelength [27]. This may be due to the presence of COOH, which occupied the adjacent position of NH2 in DOPA molecule, resulting in the poor formation of PDNPs with branched side chains which commonly existed in polydopamine [23,25,28]. Additionally, it also can be observed that the maximal fluorescence intensity was obtained when the excitation wavelength was 415nm. Thus, this excitation wavelength was used in the following Cu2+ assay.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quenching of PDNPs by Cu2+
ce:section.ce:para: Since there are many functional groups on the surface which may act as the Cu2+ binding sites, we attempted to use the prepared PDNPs for Cu2+ sensing. Dramatic fluorescence quenching was observed after the addition of 30μM Cu2+ to the PDNP solutions (Fig. 3A). The fluorescence quenching efficiency of some other metal ions was also investigated. The results are presented in Fig. 3B. It can be found that these other metal ions could not induce a remarkable quenching response. The good selectivity may be due to the higher binding affinity and faster chelating kinetics of Cu2+ with N and O atoms on the surface of the PDNPs compared to other metal ions [18,21]. Accordingly, the PDNPs could act as specific fluorescent probes for Cu2+ detection.

In order to further understand the quenching mechanism, the fluorescence intensity-based Stern–Volmer plot was investigated after the addition of Cu2+ with various concentrations. For common fluorescence quenching behavior (dynamic or static quenching), the fluorescence quenching efficiency is fitted to the Stern–Volmer equation F 0/F = K SV[Q]+1 (F 0 and F are the fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of quencher respectively, [Q] is the concentration of quencher, K SV is the Stern–Volmer constant) [29]. However, in our system, the plot of F 0/F vs. Cu2+ concentration exhibits a non-linear curve with an obvious upwards curvature (Fig. 4 ), indicating the presence of both static and dynamic quenching [29–31]. Therefore, a modified Stern–Volmer equation that could describe the quenching behavior when static quenching and dynamic quenching occurred concomitantly [29,30], F 0/F =1+(K
D
+ K
S )[Q]+(K
D
K
S )[Q]2, was used in our quenching experiments. A preferable fit of experimental data with the modified equation was obtained (R 2 =0.982, Fig. 4). Moreover, the fluorescence lifetimes in the absence and presence of Cu2+ were tested to further explore the mechanism. A decrease in fluorescence lifetime (from 3.0ns to 2.7ns) was observed after the addition of 4.0μM Cu2+, indicating the presence of dynamic quenching. The F 0/F value is larger than that of τ 0/τ at 4.0μM Cu2+ (τ 0 and τ are the lifetime in the absence and presence of Cu2+, respectively), implying that static quenching is the predominant mechanism [30,31]. The above results indicated that the present quenching system involved static quenching and dynamic quenching together. It has been reported that a contact between fluorophore and quencher is required for both static quenching and dynamic quenching [31]. In the proposed method, for the dynamic quenching process, free Cu2+ diffused to and then contacted the surface of the excited PDNPs. Upon the interaction, the PDNPs returned to the ground state without fluorescence emitting. For the static quenching process, PDNPs bound with Cu2+ to form a non-fluorescent ground-state PDNPs/Cu2+ complex [31,41].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assay performance of PDNPs for Cu2+ detection
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the above observations, PDNPs could be used as novel fluorescent probes for Cu2+ detection. Before the standard Cu2+ assay, some important parameters including the pH value of the assay buffer and the reaction time between Cu2+ and the PDNPs were optimized. As shown in Fig. 5A, at a pH value of 7.5, maximum fluorescence quenching was obtained after adding 10μM Cu2+. Additionally, in the presence of 3.0μM Cu2+, the fluorescence quenching efficiency was enhanced with increasing reaction time and tended to become constant after 10min (Fig. 5B), indicating that the proposed system possessed a rapid fluorescence response. Accordingly, a pH of 7.5 and a reaction time of 10min were used as the optimal Cu2+ assay conditions.

Various concentrations of Cu2+ were then tested using the proposed PDNP probes. The obtained fluorescence spectra are illustrated in Fig. 6A. It can be clearly seen that the fluorescence was quenched gradually with increasing Cu2+ concentration. Fig. 6B reveals the relationship between the quenching efficiency and Cu2+ concentrations. A linear correlation (R =0.991) was observed between the value of (F 0 − F)/F 0 and Cu2+ concentration in the range 0.10 to 4.0μM. The detection limit (about 0.10μM) is comparable to or lower than those of previously reported methods using phenol formaldehyde resin nanoparticle/CdTe quantum dots [19], polyethylenimine coated carbon dots [29], graphene quantum dot [41], peptide-coated CdS quantum dots [42] and NaYF4:Yb3+/Er3+ upconversion fluorescent nanoparticles [43]. It also meets the safety limit of Cu2+ permitted in drinking water (20μM) by the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency [44]. The relative standard deviations (RSDs) were 2.8%, 4.6%, 6.4% and 1.3% in three repetitive detections of 0.50, 2.5, 4.0 and 10μM Cu2+ within one working day. Moreover, we also repetitively tested Cu2+ at the same concentration in three different days. The obtained RSDs for 1.5, 3.0 and 5.0μM Cu2+ were 4.2%, 6.1% and 3.7% respectively. The small RSDs for the analyses of Cu2+ no matter in one working day or even several different days demonstrated the good stability of proposed method. Furthermore, the recovery experiments were carried out in river water to verify the applicability of the present nanoprobe. The results are listed in Table 1 . Satisfactory recoveries and RSDs were obtained using the proposed PDNPs as Cu2+ sensing probes. These results imply that the present method is likely to be capable of detection Cu2+ in real samples.

In summary, fluorescent PDNPs were facilely prepared by autopolymerization of DOPA under alkaline conditions and were applied as effective and convenient nanoprobes for label-free detection of Cu2+. Cu2+ can induce fluorescence quenching of the PDNPs. Compared with previous fluorescent nanoprobes, the PDNPs do not require further post-modification with Cu2+ specific recognition units, making the analysis easy and low cost. Additionally, this method also possesses some other advantages, including rapid detection, high sensitivity, and good selectivity. It also has promising potential applications for the detection of Cu2+ in environmental water samples. This research not only provides a novel method for Cu2+ detection, but also promotes the development of polydopamine derivative materials for chemical or biological trace analysis.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21405025), Natural Science Foundations of Guangxi Province (2014GXNSFBA118047), Guangxi Pharmaceutical Industry Talent Highland Project (1417), and Molecular Engineering of Medicinal Resources of Education Ministry (CMEMR2013-A12) is gratefully acknowledged.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Yubin Su is currently working toward the master degree at the College of Chemistry and Pharmacy, Guangxi Normal University. His major research arrears focus on the functional nanomaterials fabrication.

Bingfang Shi is an associate professor in Baise University and currently working toward the Ph.D. degree at the College of Chemistry and Pharmacy, Guangxi Normal University. His major research interests are functional nanomaterial fabrication and chemical sensing.

Suqi Liao is currently working toward the master degree at the College of Chemistry and Pharmacy, Guangxi Normal University. Her major research arrears focus on the functional nanomaterials and SERS detection.

Yingfeng Qin received her M.S. degree in Analytical Chemistry from Guangxi Normal University in 2015. Now, she works in Guangxi Normal University. Her major research arrears focus on the functional nanomaterials and biological sensing.

Liangliang Zhang received both his M.S. and Ph.D. degrees in Analytical Chemistry from Hunan University in 2009 and 2013, respectively. Now, he works in Guangxi Normal University. His major research areas include functional nanomaterial fabrication, chemical/biological sensing technology and biological imaging.
Mengjiao Huang is currently working toward the bachelor degree at the College of Chemistry and Pharmacy, Guangxi Normal University.

Shulin Zhao is a professor in Guangxi Normal University. He received his B.S. degree in Analytical Chemistry from Hunan University, and Ph.D. degree in Chemical Engineering from Sichuan University. Now, he majors in capillary electrophoresis, and chemical and biological sensing technology. He has published over 300 research papers.
